teamObjective.explanation: I am leading the first trick with a very weak hand (ranks 3-6 of spades, all non-trump). My team should preserve what little high-card potential exists and avoid handing opponents an easy high-card capture.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner (player 2) starts with 3 cards and no public actions have occurred, so I cannot infer a specific distribution. I believe they are roughly equally likely to hold a range of ranks and suits, possibly including cards that can cover a low lead.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent holds 3 cards and no cards have been played. They likely contain some higher non-trump cards or trumps (rank 7 / jokers) that could capture a low lead, but I cannot treat any specific card as known.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the single 3 of spades expends the weakest card first, keeps the slightly higher spades for later tricks, and avoids revealing or wasting any real strength. It is the most resource-preserving lead in a weak opening hand.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 6 uses my highest non-trump card prematurely. If opponents or my partner take the trick, I have less ability to contest a later lead.
The 4 is still a low card, but it forgoes the chance to dispose of the absolute weakest card now and does not materially improve the chance of winning the trick.
The 5 is closer to my top card and wastes more of my limited high-card potential on a lead that is still likely to be beaten.

risks: A low single lead can be captured easily by an opponent holding a higher non-trump or any trump.
If my partner cannot cover, the opponents gain the lead and initiative for the next trick.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Because my hand is already weak, the loss of a 3 has minimal cost. By leading the lowest card I avoid giving opponents extra information about my suit length and keep my slightly better cards for later responses.

notes: No public plays beyond the new_game event; all hidden-card beliefs remain probabilistic.
